text: Here I'll be demonstrating a case of robotic assisted right radical nephrectomy with a retrohepatic IVC tumor thrombectomy. This seventy year old gentleman presented with hematuria. Diagnostic imaging revealed a six centimeter right renal lesion with a filling defect in the inferior vena cava. It also showed extension of the tumor thrombus into the retrohepatic region.

The surgeon makes an incision in the right triangular ligament to mobilize the liver. The duodenum is caucarized, and the inferior vena cava (IVC) is skeletonized from the posterior peritoneum. The right gonadal vein and right ureter are identified and dissected. The right accessory lower pole renal artery is dissected, clipped, and divided. Gerota's fascia surrounding the right renal vein is dissected. The right border of the IVC is freed, and dissection continues on the left border, with careful ligation and division of the individual lumbar veins.

The surgeon frees the infrarenal portion of the inferior vena cava (IVC) and loops it using a VESI loop. Posterior lumbar tributaries are identified, clipped, and divided to prevent bleeding. The left renal vein is dissected, looped, and secured with a VESI loop. The right renal vein, containing the thrombus, is carefully dissected and secured using a VESI loop, ensuring all major and minor tributaries to the IVC are secured to prevent intraoperative hemorrhage.

The right renal artery is visualized in the retrocaval region, clipped but not divided. The surgeon carefully secures the short and fragile right adrenal vein to prevent bleeding. Dissection proceeds with attention to the short hepatic veins and the caudate lobe of the liver before accessing the retrohepatic portion of the IVC, avoiding the use of electrosurgical diathermy.

The surgeon reflects the caudate lobe away from the inferior vena cava (IVC), utilizing intraoperative ultrasound to assess the thrombus limits. A laparoscopic Satinsky's clamp is applied to secure the infrahepatic portion of the IVC, with a dry run being performed to ensure proper placement.

The surgeon establishes complete control around the IVC, administering 100 milliliters of mannitol intravenously. Bulldog clamps secure the IVC proximally, while a Satinsky's clamp controls the retrohepatic segment. The left renal vein is also clamped with a Bulldog clamp. A cavotomy is performed at the convergence of the right renal vein and IVC, with the incision extended cranially to reach the upper limit of the thrombus. The superior limit of the tumor thrombus is delivered through the cavotomy, revealing the orifice of the right hepatic vein.
The surgeon extends the cavotomy circumferentially to include the cuff of the IVC and right renal vein, ensuring precise control over these structures during the procedure.

The surgeon begins closure of the cavotomy at its superior limit using a continuous suture with 5-0 Prolene. Robotic instruments, including ProGrass forceps, are used to handle the cavotomy edges, leaving visible impressions from the sharp teeth of the forceps on the delicate IVC tissue.

The surgeon approaches the completion of the cavotomy closure, taking the final sutures while the surgical assistant inserts an eight French infant feeding tube pre-filled with normal saline into the cavotomy. The assistant infuses normal saline into the IVC to eliminate any remnant air bubbles, clots, or thrombi. The closure aims to be watertight, with a two-layered closure considered for a thin-walled IVC.

The surgeon releases the clamps in a cranial to caudal order. The right renal artery is clipped and divided on the right side of the IVC. A hilar lymphadenectomy is performed, and the specimen is bagged and retrieved through a fan and steel incision.
